name: The Divine Trophic Cascade
stage: variable — depends on how many gods have been removed
description:
Not a monster. Not an army. A process.

The divine trophic cascade is what happens when gods are removed from the worship ecosystem without understanding what they sustained. Each god is a keystone species — maintaining conditions that other gods and mortals depend on. Remove one and the downstream effects are specific, surprising, and delayed.

The Burn maintained thermal stability in the forge valleys. Remove it: winters get harder, crops freeze, the forge quarter goes cold. Nobody connected the colder winters to the unworship until a season later.

The Drift maintained river predictability. When its worshippers settled, the rivers shifted course. Fords moved. Trade routes became unreliable. The information network degraded.

The Weave maintained diversity. It's the starfish in the tide pool — the keystone that prevents any single force from dominating. Remove the Weave and the strongest remaining force fills the space. Usually the Silence. The mussel monoculture.

The cascade isn't triggered by a single event. It's triggered by a pattern: the rational removal of divine forces one at a time, each individual decision justified, the cumulative effect catastrophic. Aran's list in the Silence Falls — every god he diminished, every reason why, every downstream effect nobody predicted. The list gets longer every season.

The threat isn't that gods will die. The threat is that mortals will remove them without understanding what they held up — and by the time the understanding arrives, the ecosystem has already collapsed past the point of recovery.

stages.stage_1: First unworship. The Ashfield god reshaped or diminished. The technique works. Proof of concept. Nobody notices downstream effects because the Ashfield god was everyone's problem.
stages.stage_2: The technique spreads. The Burn diminished (threatening a settlement). A harvest spirit quieted (too many offerings). Each application rational. Tiya's crops freeze. Downstream effects begin but are local, attributable to 'bad weather.'
stages.stage_3: Pattern emerges. Multiple gods removed. The divine ecosystem is visibly degrading — climate shifts, trade disruption, cultural dissolution. Aran starts his list. The divine conservators form. Too late to reverse what's already been removed.
stages.stage_4: The Silence Falls. The mussel monoculture. The Silence — concept god of absence — fills every space vacated by the removed gods. A monoculture of quiet. The divine ecosystem has collapsed to a single dominant species. The rain stops coming because the forest is gone.

the_ecological_model: Remove the starfish from the tide pool: mussels take over. Remove the wolves from Yellowstone: elk multiply, vegetation collapses. Remove the gods from the worship ecosystem: the Silence fills the gap. The analogy is exact because the divine ecosystem operates on the same principles as biological ecosystems — keystone species, trophic levels, niche competition, carrying capacity. The worship economy IS an ecology. Treating it as anything else leads to the cascade.
who_triggers_it: The Unworshippers (Age of Questions). Kavan is the first. Aran is the most prolific. The Senate funds it. The divine conservators try to stop it. The irony: each individual unworship is justified. Nobody wants to live near a threatening god. The cascade isn't caused by malice. It's caused by rational actors solving rational problems without understanding the system they're operating in.
how_the_player_encounters_it: The player sees the cascade through its effects. Colder winters. Shifting rivers. Quieter towns. The world getting simpler, sparser, less alive. If the player understands divine ecology (from the Ash Tenders, from Ossa, from their own accumulated knowledge), they can trace the effects back to the removed gods. If they don't, the cascade looks like entropy — things just getting worse for no reason.

resolution_paths.prevention: Don't remove gods. Sarai's Covenant approach: reshape, don't destroy. The fermentation model.
resolution_paths.restoration: Unprecedented. Can you re-worship a god that's been unworsipped? The starfish can't be put back in the pool. But directed belief created the gods in the first place. Maybe it can recreate them. The result won't be the same god. It'll be something new wearing old clothes.
resolution_paths.adaptation: The Ember in Ash approach. The cascade completes. The old ecosystem is gone. Something new grows from a different energy source — not worship but direct human connection. Chemosynthesis of culture.
resolution_paths.acceptance: The Godless Age. The cascade completes. Humanity accepts life without gods. The Mourner prays to nothing. The empty vessel keeps its shape.
